metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the dry-run parity check to AC5, e.g. `python -m reconciler --dry-run 2>&1 | tee /tmp/after.txt` compared against a pre-captured baseline `before.txt` with `diff before.txt after.txt` (zero delta expected), and specify which output fields constitute 'identical'.
findings.finding: AC5's 'reconcile dry-run behaves identically to today' provides no concrete proving command, no baseline artifact to diff against, and no specification of what output constitutes 'identical' — making it unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reconcile dry-run behaves identically to today' with: 'Capture `reconciler --dry-run` stdout/stderr before and after the change; `diff before.txt after.txt` produces zero delta on the same test-fixture repo. Record the diff as a PR artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what 'documented' means for AC-4's cold-start window: define whether the implementation must (a) call `seed_baselines_from_snapshot()` on first always-on pass, OR (b) emit a RECON-line warning when `get_baseline()==None` is observed for a confirmed binding, OR (c) add an entry to `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` describing the one-pass warm-up — and state which option is required.
findings.finding: The cold-start fallback for `get_baseline()==None` (local-wins on first pass after upgrade from a version that never dual-wrote) is raised as a concern but left unresolved: AC-4 says 'seeded or documented' without specifying what 'documented' means operationally — no protocol is defined for detecting, logging, or bounding the warm-up window so an implementer can satisfy the AC.
findings.location: Plan section 'Cold-start note' and Acceptance Criteria AC-4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC-4: 'If seeding is not wired, `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` must describe the warm-up window and the reconciler must emit a `RECON: baseline_cold_start local_id=<id>` line to stderr whenever `get_baseline()==None` for a confirmed binding, so operators can identify the window from GHA run logs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item (or prose) requiring that tests covering the removed `baseline_dual_write=False` / `baseline_consumer_swap=False` branches and `baseline_shadow.py` machinery are identified and deleted or updated to target the now-unconditional path.
findings.finding: The plan removes the flag=False code paths (boolean threading collapsed, `baseline_shadow.py` dead machinery removed, back-out branches deleted), but no AC item or test-plan prose specifies finding and removing/updating tests that exercised those now-deleted paths. Tests that relied on `baseline_dual_write=False` or `baseline_consumer_swap=False` states — including any unit tests that mock the flag and assert the shadow/skip behavior — will either silently pass against dead code or become orphaned, masking regression coverage gaps.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Tests asserting flag=False / shadow-only behavior are removed or migrated; `grep -r baseline_dual_write\|baseline_consumer_swap\|baseline_shadow` in the test tree returns no hits after the change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_baseline_consumer_swap.py` to the edit set — delete it (or replace its flag-off cases with always-on equivalence tests) as part of this plan.
findings.finding: The plan does not mention updating or deleting `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_baseline_consumer_swap.py`, which is entirely dedicated to testing the `baseline_consumer_swap` flag (including `OutboundDiffConfig().baseline_consumer_swap is False` assertions and flag-off/flag-on branches). After the flag is hardcoded-on and the parameter removed, this test file will fail or become dead.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the file entirely, or convert the flag-off branch tests to confirm unconditional baseline consumption and remove the `OutboundDiffConfig` flag-property assertions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_file_wiring.py` (lines 203–218) to the edit set and delete the two `baseline_consumer_swap` config-wiring tests.
findings.finding: The plan does not mention `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_config_file_wiring.py`, which contains two tests (`test_baseline_consumer_swap_default_false`, `test_baseline_consumer_swap_rebar_toml_roundtrip`) that reference the `baseline_consumer_swap` config field being removed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_baseline_shadow.py` to the edit set: delete it alongside `baseline_shadow.py` (or retain both if `baseline_shadow.py` is kept).
findings.finding: The plan does not mention `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_baseline_shadow.py`, which tests `baseline_shadow.py` directly. If `baseline_shadow.py` is deleted (per AC item 3), this test file must also be deleted, but it is not enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: AC item: 'Dead shadow machinery (`baseline_shadow.py`) removed if fully unreachable' — unenumerated consumer: `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_baseline_shadow.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC-3 an explicit removal checklist: (a) the `if _dual_write:` block in `reconcile.py:938-954` is removed entirely (not just the flag read), (b) `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_baseline_shadow.py` is deleted alongside `baseline_shadow.py`, and (c) `make test` passing is the reachability proof.
findings.finding: The plan's decision rule for whether to remove `baseline_shadow.py` ('if fully dead') is not co-located with any reachability criterion, and the removal protocol is ambiguous: the plan does not specify that `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_baseline_shadow.py` (which directly imports the module) must also be deleted, nor that the dynamic `_load(...)` call site in `reconcile.py:941-954` must be removed before the module is deleted.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC-3 to: 'Dead shadow machinery (`baseline_shadow.py` + its test file `test_baseline_shadow.py`) removed; the `if _dual_write:` block at `reconcile.py:938-954` removed (not just the flag); OR a comment records why any part stays. Proof: `make test` passes and `grep -r baseline_shadow src/` returns empty.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the seed call is re-applied idempotently on every pass (or until all baselines are present) rather than once, OR add a log/alert when seeding occurs so operators can cross-check against the commit-step outcome — addressing the known clobber risk (reconcile.py:964-975) that can silently erase a one-shot seed.
findings.finding: The plan raises the cold-start seed concern and requires it be addressed (AC: 'Cold-start local-wins window is either seeded or documented'), but does not address the partial-failure resumability of the seed write itself. The existing code path (reconcile.py:964-975) explicitly warns that a commit failure leaves bindings.json on disk but NOT on the tickets branch — a subsequent 'git merge origin/tickets' can silently clobber the file, erasing seeded baselines with no operator-visible signal, causing the next pass to silently re-enter local-wins mode. The plan does not specify whether the seed is re-applied on the next pass if baselines are absent, nor does it require an alert or log when a seed is applied so operators can verify it persisted across the commit step.
findings.location: Cold-start note section; AC 'Cold-start local-wins window is either seeded or documented'; reconcile.py:964-975 (known clobber risk commentary)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the preferred resolution for the cold-start window in the plan (e.g. 'Default: add a one-paragraph code-comment in `reconcile.py` documenting the warm-up window; seeding is a stretch goal') so the executor does not face a design fork.
findings.finding: The cold-start note presents two equally-valid resolutions ('Either wire the seed … or document the one-pass warm-up window') without stating a preferred default, leaving a real design decision to the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the cold-start note: 'Default path: add a `# WARM-UP WINDOW` comment block in `reconcile.py` near the always-on call-site explaining the one-pass local-wins risk; seeding via `seed_baselines_from_snapshot()` is out of scope for this story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify parser behaviour on unknown keys (strict vs. lenient) and, if strict, add an expand-contract step (accept-then-ignore before removing) or document that rebar.toml must be updated atomically with the binary.
findings.finding: The Scope's backward-compat justification cites only bindings.json additive persistence (ADR-0026) but the What section removes schema validators for these config keys. If the parser rejects unknown keys (strict mode), any rebar.toml still containing 'baseline_dual_write' or 'baseline_consumer_swap' will fail to parse after the upgrade — a breaking config change that the 'SAFE' verdict does not acknowledge or mitigate.
findings.location: Scope section ('Backward-compat verdict: SAFE') vs. What section ('Remove both fields + validators from ReconcilerConfig')
findings.suggested_fix: If the parser is lenient (ignores unknown keys), add one sentence to Scope: 'Unknown keys are silently ignored by the parser, so rebar.toml files not yet pruned will continue to load.' If strict, split into two PRs: PR1 — deprecate validators (keys accepted but ignored); PR2 — remove fields.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CHANGELOG entry (or upgrade note in the README/operator guide) documenting the removal of `reconciler.baseline_dual_write` and `reconciler.baseline_consumer_swap`, including what to do if these keys are currently set.
findings.finding: The plan removes two named config keys (`reconciler.baseline_dual_write`, `reconciler.baseline_consumer_swap`) from the schema and parser but does not mention a changelog entry, deprecation notice, or migration guide for operators/forks that may have these keys set in their own configs — they will receive parse errors with no explanation after upgrading.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Breaking Changes` / `## Migration` entry in CHANGELOG.md: 'Remove `reconciler.baseline_dual_write` and `reconciler.baseline_consumer_swap` from `rebar.toml`; these flags are no longer recognized and must be deleted from any project config before upgrading.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require that when get_baseline() returns None and local-wins is applied, a structured log warning (or metric increment) is emitted so operators can detect and investigate the cold-start overwrite window.
findings.finding: The cold-start local-wins window (when get_baseline()==None silently treats local state as authoritative, potentially reverting a concurrent Jira edit) has no observability instrumentation. The plan's AC only requires it be 'seeded or documented' but does not require a log warning or metric when None-baseline causes a local-wins fallback, leaving operators blind to the overwrite risk.
findings.location: ## Cold-start note; AC line '- [ ] Cold-start local-wins window is either seeded or documented.'
findings.suggested_fix: In outbound_fields.py at the arbitration_prev assignment site, add: `if arbitration_prev is None: log.warning('baseline_missing_local_wins', local_id=local_id)` and add a corresponding counter metric. Reference this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify/update the backward-compat verdict: check coerce_sparse() behavior for removed keys (warns in strict=False, errors in strict=True), and either add a deprecation-alias period, document the upgrade path for repos with these keys set, or confirm strict mode is never enabled in practice.
findings.finding: The plan's backward-compat 'SAFE' verdict cites ADR-0026 to justify that removing the two config keys is safe, but ADR-0026 in the codebase is specifically about per-binding baseline semantics (absent baseline → local-wins), NOT about config-parser key compatibility. The actual parser behavior — confirmed by reading coerce_sparse() — is that after the keys are removed from _SECTIONS['reconciler'], any user rebar.toml that still contains 'baseline_dual_write' or 'baseline_consumer_swap' will trigger _warn_unknown (a WARNING in default strict=False mode; a hard ConfigError in strict=True mode). The plan removes these keys from the project's own rebar.toml but does not address parser-side backward compatibility for consumers who have these keys in their rebar.toml.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Backward-compat verdict: SAFE. Baseline is persisted additively into bindings.json (no store-version bump; older binaries ignore the extra keys, per ADR-0026)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add both keys to `_ALIASES` (mapping to themselves as no-ops) for one release cycle, or document in the plan that any user rebar.toml with these keys will emit a one-time warning and must be manually cleaned up. If strict mode is never used in practice, explicitly note that in the backward-compat justification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` to the edit set: remove or archive the convergence-rollout section (lines ~317–350 and ~470–485) that documents the now-retired flags.
findings.finding: `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` contains an entire section ('Convergence rollout: the baseline consumer swap') at line 317, plus flip-procedure prose at lines 323/335/344/347 and rollback instructions at lines 472/483, all referencing the two flags being retired. The plan does not mention updating the docs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan to the Scope section (e.g., 'revert commit X restores the flags; re-add the two rebar.toml lines to re-gate behaviour') or confirm a one-commit revert is sufficient and document it as such.
findings.finding: The plan declares the change 'SAFE' and justified but provides no explicit rollback plan. T4 requires a reversibility mechanism, not merely an acknowledgement of safety. If hardcoded always-on surfaces unexpected production behaviour (e.g., baseline-None local-wins storm before cold-start window closes), operators have no documented path to revert.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Rollback: revert this commit. The removed rebar.toml keys are re-added and the gated code paths are restored; no bindings.json migration is required because baseline data is additive and backward-compatible.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the flag-retirement decision and its safety rationale (including the cold-start warm-up window) in a new or updated ADR (or CLAUDE.md section), so future maintainers understand why no rollback flag exists and what the upgrade path is for fresh repos.
findings.finding: The decision to permanently retire the rollout-flag back-out path (hardcoding always-on and removing `baseline_shadow.py`) is an architectural closure that future maintainers need to understand, but no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is planned to capture it.
findings.location: ## Why; ## What — baseline_shadow.py removal; Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or append to an existing convergence-rollout ADR) covering: (1) why the flags are permanently retired, (2) the safety justification (single advisory-locked GHA host, additive bindings.json, ADR-0026), and (3) the cold-start local-wins window and the decision to document rather than seed it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure to the plan (e.g., 'revert this commit and redeploy; rebar.toml and binary must be reverted together') and note the deploy-ordering constraint so config and binary changes are always applied atomically.
findings.finding: The plan removes both feature flags and their rebar.toml entries but specifies no rollback procedure. If a bug is discovered post-deploy, the only undo is a code revert + redeploy, yet the plan is silent on this path. Additionally, if binary and config are rolled back independently (old binary restored without restoring the two rebar.toml lines), the old binary's schema validator will reject the config — a partial-rollback failure mode.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — no rollback AC present; 'Remove the two lines from rebar.toml (:65, :70)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: '- [ ] Rollback path documented: revert the commit restoring both the schema fields and the rebar.toml lines; binary and config are always deployed together so partial rollback is safe.' Include a note that the two rebar.toml removals and the schema removal ship in the same deployment unit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the baseline entry retirement policy in the plan: either (a) baseline entries are pruned when a ticket leaves scope (specify where), or (b) accumulation is accepted and its bounds are justified (e.g., bounded by total-tickets-ever-seen which is acceptable for this use case).
findings.finding: Baseline entries written into bindings.json are persisted additively with no defined retirement/cleanup path. Entries for deleted or closed tickets will accumulate indefinitely, and the plan has no AC or implementation note addressing this lifecycle gap.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Baseline is persisted additively into bindings.json (no store-version bump)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item requiring a unit test for `outbound_fields.py` that exercises `get_baseline()==None` under always-consume and asserts the fallback behavior (local-wins result, log warning, or seed trigger) is explicit and correct.
findings.finding: The cold-start scenario (`get_baseline()==None` after hardcoding always-consume) is a new failure-path introduced by making the consume unconditional, but no test coverage is specified for it. AC item 4 says the window is 'seeded or documented' but does not require a test that exercises the None-baseline code path in `outbound_fields.py:418-419` and asserts the 'local-wins' fallback behaves safely (e.g., does not silently drop a concurrent Jira edit without logging).
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A unit test covers `binding_store.get_baseline(local_id)` returning None in the unconditional consume path and asserts the fallback outcome (e.g., `arbitration_prev=None` produces local-wins with a logged warning).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `outbound_fields.py:380` (the `baseline_consumer_swap: bool = False` parameter declaration) to the explicit edit set alongside the 418-419 body lines.
findings.finding: The plan cites `outbound_fields.py:418-419` (the conditional body) for hardcoding but does not name line 380, which is the function-parameter declaration `baseline_consumer_swap: bool = False`. That parameter also needs to be removed from the function signature (and its call sites at `outbound_differ.py:626,805` updated to not pass the kwarg), but those call-site edits are named; the parameter declaration line is not.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `baseline_consumer_swap` parameter from `_diff_fields`'s signature at line 380 and drop the kwarg at the two named call sites (`outbound_differ.py:626`, `outbound_differ.py:805`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify or create a live parent ticket (e.g., a post-rollout cleanup task or an open maintenance epic) whose declared scope explicitly includes retiring `baseline_dual_write`/`baseline_consumer_swap` and removing `baseline_shadow.py`, then confirm this leaf's What/ACs are a subset of that parent's plan.
findings.finding: The leaf proposes retiring both rollout config flags and removing associated dead code — work that is absent from the declared parent epics' plans. Both ace2-f784-4b19-4f22 (adept-hedge-stain) and 3006-e198-13db-4e1f (file-lens-jewel) are closed and neither contains a flag-retirement step; a118 (lofty-beech-twang), the direct predecessor, scoped only adding and flipping the flags. The leaf delivers something no live parent plan contains, violating the leaf-must-be-a-subset-of-parent contract.
findings.location: Plan 'Why' / 'What' sections; ace2-f784-4b19-4f22 Band B AC; 3006-e198-13db-4e1f Scope boundary and operational-tail carve-out; a118 Scope/ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) create a new open task/epic for 'post-rollout config flag retirement and dead-code removal' with this cleanup in scope, parent this leaf to it, and confirm the leaf is a subset; or (b) declare this plan as a standalone unparented task (if the project supports that) and remove any implicit parent claim to ace2-f784 or 3006-e198.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
